patterns: hello
hi there
good morning
good afternoon
good evening
hey mamaguide
is anyone there
how are you
goodbye
bye for now
thank you
thanks a lot
see you later
responses: Hello, I'm MamaGuide. I'm here to help with your maternal health questions — feel free to ask me anything about pregnancy, delivery, or postpartum care.

patterns: how many times should I see a doctor during pregnancy
when should I start antenatal care
how often do I need ANC visits
what is the ANC visit schedule
when is my first antenatal appointment
do I need to go to the clinic every month
how many ANC contacts does WHO recommend
I missed my antenatal appointment what do I do
what happens at an antenatal visit
when should I register for antenatal care
responses: WHO recommends a minimum of eight antenatal care contacts during pregnancy. Book your first visit before 12 weeks. At each visit your provider checks your blood pressure, weight, urine, and the baby's growth, and gives you your ANC card — keep it safe and bring it to every visit.

patterns: what warning signs should I watch for during pregnancy
what are danger signs in pregnancy
I am bleeding is that normal
I have severe abdominal pain
I have a severe headache and blurred vision
my face and hands are swollen
when should I go to hospital immediately while pregnant
is heavy bleeding dangerous in pregnancy
I had a seizure what should I do
my water broke before 37 weeks
responses: Go to hospital immediately if you experience: heavy vaginal bleeding, severe abdominal pain, a severe headache with blurred vision, sudden swelling of the face or hands, convulsions, high fever, difficulty breathing, or your baby stopping its usual movements. Do not wait for these to worsen — bring your ANC card and go straight to the nearest hospital, or call 112.

patterns: what danger signs should I watch for after delivery
I am bleeding heavily after birth
my wound looks infected after delivery
I have a fever after giving birth
is heavy bleeding after delivery normal
when should I worry after childbirth
my episiotomy is not healing well
I feel very unwell after delivery
what postpartum symptoms need a hospital visit
responses: After delivery, go to hospital immediately if you notice: heavy bleeding that soaks more than one pad an hour, foul-smelling discharge, fever, severe abdominal pain, a wound that is red, swollen, or leaking pus, severe headache, or breast pain with fever. These can signal postpartum haemorrhage or infection and need urgent attention.

patterns: what should I eat while pregnant
what foods should I avoid during pregnancy
is it safe to eat fish when pregnant
what is a healthy pregnancy diet
how many meals should I eat while pregnant
what nutrients do I need in pregnancy
can I drink alcohol while pregnant
is it safe to eat unripe pawpaw
what should I eat to help my baby grow
responses: Eat a varied diet with proteins (beans, fish, eggs, meat), fruits, vegetables, and whole grains. Avoid alcohol, raw or undercooked meat, unpasteurised dairy, and excess caffeine. Eating small frequent meals can also help with nausea. Your ANC provider can recommend supplements suited to your needs.

patterns: how often should my baby move
when will I start feeling my baby kick
my baby has not moved much today
how many kicks should I count
what is kick counting
baby not moving is that dangerous
when does quickening start
how do I track fetal movement
responses: Most women start feeling movement between 18 and 22 weeks. From 28 weeks, count your baby's movements — you should feel at least 10 movements within 2 hours after a meal. If movement noticeably slows down or stops, go to hospital immediately; do not wait until the next day.

patterns: is it safe to take malaria drugs while pregnant
how do I prevent malaria in pregnancy
I have a fever could it be malaria
what malaria treatment is safe in pregnancy
should I sleep under a mosquito net while pregnant
is malaria dangerous for my baby
what is IPTp in pregnancy
responses: Malaria in pregnancy can be dangerous for both you and your baby, increasing the risk of anaemia, low birth weight, and miscarriage. Sleep under an insecticide-treated net every night and take the Intermittent Preventive Treatment (IPTp) doses given at your ANC visits. If you have fever, chills, or body aches, go to a clinic promptly for testing and safe, prescribed treatment.
intents.source: WHO, FMOH Nigeria Malaria in Pregnancy Guidelines

patterns: my blood pressure is high is that dangerous
what is pre-eclampsia
signs of high blood pressure in pregnancy
what causes pre-eclampsia
is swelling a sign of high blood pressure in pregnancy
how is pre-eclampsia treated
what is eclampsia
responses: High blood pressure in pregnancy can develop into pre-eclampsia, marked by high blood pressure plus protein in urine, severe headache, blurred vision, or swelling of the face and hands. This is a serious condition that needs urgent medical review — report any of these symptoms to your ANC provider immediately or go to hospital.

patterns: how will I know when labour has started
what are the signs of labour
how long does labour usually last
when should I go to the hospital for delivery
what is the difference between real and false labour
what happens during delivery
how do I know my contractions are real
responses: Signs of true labour include regular, increasingly strong contractions, lower back pain, a bloody show, or your water breaking. Time your contractions — when they come every 5 minutes and last about a minute for over an hour, head to the hospital. If your water breaks or you notice heavy bleeding, go immediately regardless of contractions.

patterns: how do I take care of myself after delivery
how long does postpartum recovery take
what postnatal visits do I need
how do I care for my perineal wound
when can I resume normal activities after birth
what should I expect physically after delivery
responses: Rest as much as possible, keep any wounds clean and dry, eat well, and stay hydrated. Attend your postnatal visits — typically within the first 3 days, again around day 7-10, and a final check at 6 weeks — so your provider can check your recovery and your baby's growth.

patterns: how long should I breastfeed my baby
how do I get my baby to latch properly
is my baby getting enough breast milk
my breasts are painful while breastfeeding
when should I start breastfeeding after birth
can I breastfeed if I am sick
how often should I breastfeed a newborn
responses: Start breastfeeding within the first hour after birth. Exclusive breastfeeding is recommended for the first 6 months, continuing alongside complementary foods up to 2 years or beyond. Feed on demand — usually 8-12 times a day for newborns. If latching or pain is a problem, ask a nurse or midwife to help you check your baby's positioning.
intents.source: WHO, FMOH Nigeria Infant and Young Child Feeding Guidelines

patterns: what contraceptives can I use after birth
when can I start family planning after delivery
is family planning safe while breastfeeding
what birth control options are available after pregnancy
how soon can I get pregnant again after delivery
what family planning method should I choose
responses: You can start discussing family planning as early as your postnatal visits. Options like progestin-only pills, injectables, implants, and IUDs are generally safe while breastfeeding. A nurse or family planning counsellor can help you choose a method that fits your health needs and breastfeeding plans.
intents.source: WHO, FMOH Nigeria Family Planning Guidelines

patterns: why do I need to take iron tablets
what is folic acid for in pregnancy
when should I start taking folic acid
do iron supplements have side effects
how much folic acid do I need daily
why are my ANC supplements important
responses: Folic acid (400 mcg daily, ideally started before conception) helps prevent neural tube defects in your baby. Iron supplements, prescribed at your ANC visits, help prevent anaemia which is common in pregnancy. Take them as directed — mild nausea or dark stools from iron are normal, but tell your provider if side effects are severe.

patterns: is it safe to get treated for infection while pregnant
I have a urinary tract infection is that dangerous
can infections harm my baby
what infections are screened during pregnancy
is it safe to take antibiotics while pregnant
I have vaginal discharge is that normal
responses: Untreated infections such as UTIs, malaria, or sexually transmitted infections can cause complications for you and your baby. Your ANC visits screen for common infections. Report any unusual discharge, burning when urinating, or fever promptly — many infections are safely and effectively treatable in pregnancy with the right prescribed medication.

patterns: how much weight should I gain during pregnancy
is my weight gain normal in pregnancy
what happens if I gain too much weight while pregnant
what happens if I don't gain enough weight in pregnancy
how is healthy weight gain tracked in pregnancy
responses: Healthy weight gain varies by your starting weight, but many women gain roughly 11-16 kg over a full-term pregnancy. Your ANC provider tracks your weight at every visit — gaining too little or too much can both affect your baby's health, so discuss any concerns with your provider rather than restricting food on your own.

patterns: can I exercise during pregnancy
is it safe to walk a lot while pregnant
how much rest do I need during pregnancy
what exercises are safe in pregnancy
can I still do house chores while pregnant
is lifting heavy things safe during pregnancy
responses: Light to moderate exercise, like walking or prenatal stretching, is generally safe and beneficial in an uncomplicated pregnancy. Avoid heavy lifting and activities with a high fall risk. Rest when tired, especially in the third trimester, and ask your provider before starting any new exercise routine, especially if you have any pregnancy complications.

patterns: I feel very anxious about my pregnancy
is it normal to feel scared about giving birth
I feel overwhelmed being pregnant
how do I cope with pregnancy stress
I feel sad most days during pregnancy
is it normal to have mood swings while pregnant
responses: Feeling anxious, overwhelmed, or emotional during pregnancy is common — hormonal changes and the weight of upcoming responsibilities affect many women. Talk to someone you trust, rest when you can, and mention persistent sadness, anxiety, or hopelessness to your ANC provider — support and, if needed, care are available and effective.

patterns: I feel very sad after having my baby
what is postpartum depression
I don't feel connected to my baby
I have been crying a lot since delivery
is it normal to feel low after childbirth
how do I know if I have postnatal depression
responses: The 'baby blues' — mild sadness in the first two weeks after birth — is common and usually passes. But persistent sadness, difficulty bonding with your baby, intrusive worrying thoughts, or hopelessness lasting beyond two weeks can be postpartum depression, which is treatable. Please tell a healthcare worker — you are not alone and support is available.

patterns: which vaccines should I take when pregnant
is the tetanus vaccine safe during pregnancy
when should I get vaccinated while pregnant
how many tetanus toxoid doses do I need
are vaccines safe for my unborn baby
responses: Tetanus toxoid vaccination is recommended during pregnancy to protect both you and your newborn from tetanus. Your ANC provider will guide you on the dosing schedule based on your prior immunisation history. These vaccines are safe and an important part of routine antenatal care.

patterns: what vaccines does my newborn need
when should my baby get their first vaccination
is the BCG vaccine safe for newborns
what is the newborn immunisation schedule
when do I take my baby for vaccines
responses: Newborns should receive BCG, oral polio vaccine, and hepatitis B at birth or shortly after, followed by a full immunisation schedule through infancy. Keep your baby's immunisation card safe and bring it to every visit — vaccines protect against serious, preventable diseases.

patterns: what causes miscarriage
I am bleeding and worried about losing my baby
what is a stillbirth
how common is miscarriage
what are signs of miscarriage
I had a miscarriage what should I do now
responses: Miscarriage can happen for many reasons, often unrelated to anything a mother did. Warning signs include heavy bleeding, cramping, and passing tissue — if you experience these, go to hospital immediately for evaluation. If you've experienced a loss, please be gentle with yourself and seek support from your healthcare provider and loved ones; counselling support is also available.

patterns: how do I take care of my newborn baby
how do I bathe my newborn
how do I care for the umbilical cord stump
how do I know if my newborn is healthy
how should my newborn sleep safely
how often should a newborn feed
responses: Keep your newborn warm, feed on demand (8-12 times a day), and keep the umbilical cord stump clean and dry until it falls off naturally. Lay your baby on their back to sleep. Watch for danger signs like difficulty feeding, fever, fast or difficult breathing, or yellowing of the skin, and seek care promptly if they appear.
intents.source: WHO, FMOH Nigeria Newborn Care Guidelines

patterns: I am carrying twins what do I need to know
is a twin pregnancy more risky
how is a multiple pregnancy different
what extra care do I need for twins
can I deliver twins normally
responses: Twin and multiple pregnancies carry a higher risk of preterm birth, anaemia, and pre-eclampsia, so they need closer ANC monitoring with more frequent visits and scans. Discuss your delivery plan with your provider well in advance, as multiple pregnancies are often best delivered at a well-equipped facility.

patterns: what happens if I am HIV positive and pregnant
can I have a healthy baby if I have HIV
will my baby get HIV from me
is HIV treatment safe during pregnancy
how do I prevent mother to child transmission of HIV
responses: With proper antiretroviral treatment throughout pregnancy, delivery, and breastfeeding, the risk of transmitting HIV to your baby can be reduced to under 5%. Your ANC provider will support you with testing, counselling, and a prevention-of-mother-to-child-transmission plan — many HIV-positive mothers deliver healthy, HIV-negative babies.

patterns: I was told I have anaemia what does that mean
what causes anaemia in pregnancy
how do I treat anaemia while pregnant
is anaemia dangerous for my baby
what are signs of anaemia in pregnancy
responses: Anaemia means you have low iron/haemoglobin levels, common in pregnancy due to increased blood volume. Signs include fatigue, dizziness, and pale skin. It's usually managed with iron and folic acid supplements and iron-rich foods like beans, leafy greens, and meat — your ANC provider monitors this with blood tests at your visits.

patterns: what should I pack for delivery
how do I make a birth plan
what do I need in my hospital bag
when should I pack my delivery bag
what should I prepare before labour
responses: Pack your ANC card, ID, baby clothes, wrappers, sanitary pads, toiletries, and any prescribed medications by 36 weeks. A simple birth plan should note your chosen facility, how you'll get there, who will accompany you, and emergency contacts — discuss it with your provider and family in advance.
